step_by_step_reasoning: The transcript describes a migration from IBM Netezza on-premises to Amazon Redshift. The architecture involves an initial bulk seeding phase using AWS Snowball and daily incremental ingestion of 100 GB via AWS Storage Gateway and Direct Connect. Data lands in an S3 Landing Zone. Processing is managed by an ELT pipeline driven by Postgres RDS (storing metadata and scripts) and executed on EC2 instances. The cleansed data goes to S3 Transformation Zone, then loaded to Redshift. Redshift Spectrum publishes processed data to an S3 Publish Zone, which archives to S3 Glacier. Analysts access dashboards and run ad-hoc queries directly via Redshift or the Publish bucket.

graph.name: Netezza to Redshift Migration for Life Sciences
graph.notes: HIPAA-compliant data platform migrating legacy Netezza to Redshift. Processes 100GB/day with automated S3 lifecycle transitions to Glacier.

nodes.name: AWS Snowball
nodes.notes: Used for initial 20 TB database seeding.

nodes.name: AWS Storage Gateway
nodes.notes: NFS volumes for daily 100 GB delta increments.

nodes.name: Amazon RDS PostgreSQL
nodes.notes: Stores ELT metadata and transformation scripts.

nodes.name: EC2 Processing Nodes
nodes.notes: Executes quality cleansing and ELT transformations.

nodes.name: Amazon Redshift
nodes.notes: Core data warehouse supporting Redshift Spectrum.

nodes.name: S3 Publish Zone
nodes.notes: Stores finalized dashboard data. Retention: 1 year, 1 month.

edges.notes: Seeded data loaded into landing bucket.
Data sent via NFS over Direct Connect.
Data written to S3 landing bucket.
EC2 writes cleansed data to transformation zone.
Redshift Spectrum publishes data to publish bucket.
